one_line_takeaway: Few-shot example-selection strategies (mistake-driven LFM, similarity-driven LFNN, and their combinations) can meaningfully improve LLM vulnerability-detection performance on Python/JavaScript-heavy datasets (NodeMedic, SVENP) but show limited, inconsistent, or even bias-inducing effects on C/C++-heavy datasets (PrimeVul, SVENC, DiverseVul), suggesting prompt-only interventions have a ceiling that likely requires fine-tuning or retraining for harder languages.
key_finding: Effectiveness of few-shot selection is highly dataset- and model-dependent, not uniform. On NodeMedic, LFM and LFNN both substantially beat ZS/R-FS baselines (e.g., Gemma LFNN F1=0.854 vs ZS F1=0.574; Gemma LFM reaches recall=1.000, F1=0.840; Qwen LFM/LFNN F1=0.837/0.814). On SVENP, ZS is already strong (Qwen ZS precision=0.965; GPT-5-mini ZS F1=0.780) and LFNN pushes F1 further (GPT-5-mini F1=0.889, Qwen F1=0.775; Gemma F1 0.565->0.625). On DiverseVul and PrimeVul, Qwen's ZS baseline collapses (F1=0.000, predicting all-negative), while default LFM (opt=incorrect) collapses to the opposite failure mode (F1=0.667 via recall=1.000/accuracy=0.500, i.e., all-positive predictions) -- a bias the paper explicitly attributes to LFM's inductive bias toward the model's mistake class. LFNN degrades sharply for Qwen on PrimeVul (F1=0.104) and SVENC (F1=0.075) despite succeeding on NodeMedic/SVENP. Combined Methods are generally more balanced/robust than either component alone (e.g., on DiverseVul with Gemma, CM1/CM2/CM3 reach F1=0.658/0.667/0.663 vs. LFM's 0.114 and LFNN's 0.524; on SVENP, CM2 with GPT-5-mini reaches the dataset's top F1=0.892), though gains are not universal. The LFM variant ablation (Table II) shows the stacked/single-iteration default is a consistent bias-amplifier toward recall, while unstacked multi-iteration variants' success depends unpredictably on whether they learn from incorrect, correct, or 'gray' examples -- on PrimeVul the two models even show opposite optimal settings (Qwen best with opt=incorrect, Gemma best with opt=correct).
core_contribution: The paper studies black-box, prompt-based few-shot example selection for LLM-based source-code vulnerability detection. It introduces two algorithms: Learn-from-Mistakes (LFM), which iteratively scans a labeled training set and selects examples the target LLM consistently gets wrong (or, in variants, consistently right, or 'gray'/inconsistent) to build a static few-shot set; and Learn-from-Nearest-Neighbors (LFNN), which uses a code embedding model (Salesforce/SFR-Embedding-Code-400M R) and cosine similarity to retrieve query-specific nearest-neighbor examples from the training set. It then defines three ways of combining LFM and LFNN (Method 1: union of a query-agnostic LFM set with query-specific LFNN neighbors; Method 2: use LFNN neighbors as LFM's initial seed set per query; Method 3: run LFM once query-agnostically, then re-run LFM restricted to the LFNN-retrieved subset, seeded with the first LFM output) to build query-specific few-shot sets. All methods are evaluated as prompt-engineering interventions with no fine-tuning or retraining of the underlying LLMs.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker-defender threat model; this is a detection-capability study. The 'threat' being addressed is the practical deployment gap for LLM-based vulnerability detectors: baseline (zero-shot/random few-shot) precision/recall/F1 for detecting vulnerable vs. non-vulnerable code (covering CWE classes and, via the NodeMedic dataset, Node.js command-injection/code-execution taint flows) remains too low for reliable use, and the paper investigates whether smarter in-context example selection can close that gap without costly fine-tuning.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) function-level vulnerability detection has inherent limitations relative to repository-level detection; (2) despite using PrimeVul specifically to mitigate label noise/duplication/leakage issues documented in prior work, other datasets used (DiverseVul, SVEN, NodeMedic) were not similarly vetted for these issues; (3) standard deviations across the 5 seeded runs were omitted from Table I 'because of space constraints'; (4) GPT-5-mini results are from a single run only, and the paper notes the API's seed parameter does not guarantee deterministic outputs across backend updates; (5) the paper explicitly states no single selection strategy (LFM, LFNN, or any combined method) was found to be universally superior across models/datasets/languages -- the improvements are heterogeneous and, in several cases (e.g., accuracy near 0.500 despite high F1 due to recall-driven bias), can be misleading if F1 is read in isolation from accuracy.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (7,578 training samples/3,789 pairs, 112 unique CWEs; test set downsampled to 200 balanced samples, 58 common CWEs)
DiverseVul (330,492 unpaired samples, 150 unique CWEs; downsampled to 200 train/300 test balanced samples, 114 common CWEs)
SVEN (1,440 train/166 validation total; split into SVENC: C/C++, 756 train/90 validation, 7 CWEs; SVENP: Python, 684 train/76 validation, 4 CWEs)
NodeMedic (1,506 train/189 test Node.js package samples; taint-flow vulnerability labels from the NodeMedic-FINE dynamic analysis tool, obtained directly from its authors)
quantitative_results: NodeMedic: Gemma LFNN F1=0.854 vs ZS F1=0.574; Gemma LFM recall=1.000, F1=0.840; Qwen LFM F1=0.837, LFNN F1=0.814; GPT-5-mini R-FS F1=0.860 (already strong baseline)
SVENP: Qwen ZS precision=0.965; GPT-5-mini ZS F1=0.780; LFNN raises GPT-5-mini F1 to 0.889, Qwen F1 to 0.775; Gemma LFNN F1 0.565->0.625, accuracy 0.587->0.634; CM2 GPT-5-mini F1=0.892 (dataset best)

DiverseVul: Qwen ZS F1=0.000 (all-negative); Qwen LFM F1=0.667 via recall=1.000/accuracy=0.500 (all-positive); Gemma LFM F1=0.114 (poor); Gemma Combined Methods CM1/CM2/CM3 F1=0.658/0.667/0.663, accuracy=0.548/0.515/0.499

PrimeVul/SVENC: Qwen ZS F1=0.000 on both (all-negative); Qwen LFM F1=0.667 on both (all-positive, accuracy=0.500 misleading); Qwen LFNN F1=0.104 (PrimeVul) and 0.075 (SVENC), a sharp degradation; Gemma SVENC CM2 F1=0.671 (best for that model)

LFM variant ablation (Table II): default stacked/1-iteration (S-1x, opt=incorrect) induces perfect Qwen recall=1.000 four times across datasets; unstacked 1-iteration on NodeMedic catastrophically fails (Gemma accuracy=0.273, F1=0.003); on PrimeVul, opt=incorrect vs opt=correct give opposite optimal results for Qwen (F1=0.621 vs 0.062) and Gemma (F1=0.061 vs 0.626)
baselines_compared: Random few-shot (R-FS): 20 randomly drawn vulnerable+non-vulnerable examples
GPT-5-mini (closed-source) used partly as a high-quality reference point to contextualize open-model (Gemma, Qwen) behavior, not as a prior-published SOTA baseline
